My model’s performance is measured using three key metrics. R-squared tells us how much of the profit changes(variance) the model can explain, with 1 being perfect and 0 being useless. MAE, or Mean Absolute Error, shows the average difference between predicted and actual profit — basically how much the model is off on average. RMSE, or Root Mean Squared Error, is similar but punishes big mistakes more, so it highlights the larger errors. Finally, by comparing the R-squared on training and test data, we can check if the model is overfitting — meaning it memorized the data instead of truly learning patterns.


Quick Notes for Presentation (Beginner-Friendly)

R² (R-squared)

Shows how much your model explains the changes in profit.

1 = perfect, 0 = useless.

Compare Train vs Test to see if the model is overfitting.

MAE (Mean Absolute Error)

Average difference between what the model predicts and the actual value.

Example: If MAE = 50 → predictions are off by $50 on average.

Doesn’t care if the error is too big or too small — all errors count equally.

RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)

Similar to MAE but big mistakes hurt more.

Example: If RMSE = 70 → large errors are pulling the average up.

Overfitting

Model does too well on training data but worse on test data.

Bad: Train R² >> Test R²

Good: Train R² ≈ Test R²

💡 Easy way to remember:

R² → “how much I know”

MAE → “average oops”

RMSE → “average oops, big disasters hurt more”

Key Regression Metrics (Predicting Continuous Values)

    Mean Absolute Error (MAE): The average of the absolute differences between predictions and actual values; robust to outliers.
    Mean Squared Error (MSE): The average of the squared differences, heavily penalizing large errors.
    Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): The square root of MSE, putting the metric in the same units as the target variable.
    R-squared (R^2): Represents the proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the model (0 to 1).
    Adjusted R^2: Modifies  based on the number of predictors, reducing overestimation. 

Metric 	 Purpose	                    Sensitivity to Outliers
MAE	 Average magnitude of error		Low
MSE	 Average squared error	        	High
RMSE	 Average error (same units)		High
	 Goodness of fit	                 	N/A
Adj. R^2	 Fit adjusted for complexity	 	N/A

When to Use Which:
   * Use RMSE or MSE when large errors are particularly undesirable.
    *Use MAE when you want an easily interpretable, robust measure of average error.
    *Use Adjusted
    when building multiple linear regression models to avoid overestimation from adding unnecessary variables.
